nativeRange.habitat: Exposed seaward reefs with hard bottoms in areas of strong current, typically 2–4 m deep (recorded 2–31 m); commonly forms small-to-large aggregations that share refuges in the reef matrix

decorPreferences: open sand bed at least 2 inches deep for burrowing
live rock with crevices and overhangs for shelter
ample open swimming space in the water column
tight-fitting lid required to prevent jumping
moderate to strong water movement preferred

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild feeding on zooplankton from the water column above the reef. In captivity generally accepts mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafoods, finely chopped krill, and frozen carnivore preparations. One to two small feedings per day are generally advised to mimic continuous planktivory.

summary: The Zebra Barred Dartfish (Ptereleotris zebra) is a peaceful, reef-safe species from the Indo-Pacific, recognizable by a pale green body crossed by numerous fine orange vertical bars. Generally considered beginner-friendly, it tends to do best in groups of two to five, and a tight-fitting lid is essential — this species is a well-documented jumper.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in an established reef or fish-only aquarium of at least roughly 120 L (about 30 US gal), though a longer footprint of around 120 cm (48 in) is commonly recommended to provide the open swimming space this species tends to need. A deep open sand bed and live rock with crevices for refuge are generally considered important — multiple individuals tend to share the same shelter in the wild.

Widely regarded as a jumper that startles easily; a tight-fitting lid with no gaps is generally considered essential.

Unlike pair-forming dartfish (e.g. Scissortail and Helfrich's), this species tends to be a shoaling planktivore that does best in groups of 2–5 introduced simultaneously. Single individuals can be kept but have been reported to be more reclusive.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species is not documented in commonly available hobbyist sources. In the wild, individuals have been reported to form mixed-sex aggregations over the reef, but no established aquarium breeding protocol exists in the typical hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Per-species product page supplied sizing (4 in / ≈10 cm), minimum tank (30 US gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025), reef compatibility, peaceful temperament, carnivorous diet, easy care level, and prose on grouping (small groups or pairs preferred) and jumping risk.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Gobiiformes / Microdesmidae, Fowler 1938), max length 12.0 cm SL, native range (Red Sea and islands in the western Indian Ocean to the Line and Marquesas Islands, north to the Ryukyu Islands, south to the southern Great Barrier Reef; Mariana and Marshall Islands), exposed-seaward-reef habitat at 2–31 m (typically 2–4 m), wild temperature 22–28°C, planktivorous feeding, IUCN Least Concern (assessed 12 March 2015), and small-to-large aggregations sharing refuges all cross-checked. Used in place of the assigned secondary URL (which was a collections-page 404).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Independent third source. Confirmed 4-inch (≈10 cm) maximum, 30 US gal minimum with recommendation for a 48-inch-long tank, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful temperament, reef compatibility, carnivorous diet, group-of-2–5 social preference, and jumping/lid requirement. Reported a slightly higher temperature ceiling of 80°F (26.7°C), which was averaged into the 23–27°C range used here.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning of this species is not documented in typical hobby literature.
